data.title: GATX Corporation: Still Not Hitching My Future To This Name
data.description:
In May 2024, one company that I decided to rate a "Hold" was GATX Corporation (GATX). The business, which owns a large fleet of rail cars, is certainly the kind of firm that interests me. Having said that, while the overall trend of financial performance for the business was decent, shares were not cheap enough to justify a bullish outlook. Whenever I rate a company a "Hold," I'm making the claim that shares are likely to perform more or less similarly to the broader market for the foreseeable future. And I would say we have had that. Since the time that the article was published, the stock has been up 26.1%. That's a bit above the 23.1% increase seen by the S&P 500 over the same period. But considering how much time has passed, I wouldn't call it a significant disparity.

Looking at the picture today, I see that the firm is cheaper on a forward basis than it was back then. But between how it is priced on an absolute basis and how it is priced compared to other similar enterprises, I'm not ready to upgrade it at this time. This is even after management announced earlier this year a rather large investment that should, in the long run, bode well for shareholders. Given these thoughts, I believe that maintaining the company as a "Hold" candidate is the right choice at this time.

Taking a ride on GATX

Given how much time has elapsed, I think it would be helpful to dig a bit into the overall core operations of GATX. According to management, the company leases, operates, manages, and re-markets certain rail-related assets. It is a pretty large firm. At the end of the most recent quarter, which should be the second quarter of the 2025 fiscal year, its Rail North America fleet consisted of 102,317 railcars. These boasted a 99.2% utilization rate. That's about as high as you can get.

This does not include boxcars the company does not factor them into these members. Those totaled 7,621 at the end of the most recent quarter. The reason why management does not include these in the other count is simply because they seem to be in the process of gradually phasing out this part of the business. A year earlier, for instance, the company had 8,990 boxcars to its name. But since then, it has scrapped a total of 1,361 while selling off another 35. It added only 27 during this time. For its international operations, which involve its business activities in Europe and India, the business has 30,492 railcars.

There is yet a third operating segment that the company has. But this is quite small in the grand scheme of things. For context, during the second quarter of the 2025 fiscal year, this segment, known as Engine Leasing, accounted for only 3.4% of the firm's overall revenue, so it barely deserves a mention. The only reason why I discuss it, however, is that it generates a lot of profitability. In the first half of this year, it was responsible for 20.5% of total segment profits. This part of the business consists of certain joint venture ownerships, including a manufacturer of commercial aircraft engines.

Over the last couple of years, we have seen solid growth for the business. In 2024, revenue was $1.59 billion. That's up from the $1.41 billion the company reported the previous year. This was driven by growth across all three of its operating segments.

Rail North America, for instance, saw revenue grow 11.8% from $982.7 million to $1.10 billion. This was thanks to a jump in lease revenue, with management attributing this to a combination of higher lease rates and more railcars on lease. It appears as though most of the change would have had to have been from higher rates. I say this because the number of railcars that were active on an average basis during the year 2024 came in only 1.2% above what the company saw the previous year.

This was not all. For its international operations, the company saw revenue expand from $309.5 million to $350.3 million. An increase in the average active rail car of 1.7% certainly helped. But obviously, higher rates did the heavy lifting. And finally, the Engine Leasing segment reported a rise in revenue from $77.2 million to $97.1 million. This was entirely because non-dedicated engine revenue rose in response to aircraft spare engines acquired during the year.

With revenue rising, profitability for the business also increased. Net income grew from $259.2 million to $284.2 million. We saw operating cash flow jump from $520.4 million to $602.1 million. If we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get an increase from $526.8 million to $603 million. And finally, EBITDA for the company expanded from $782.6 million to $899.1 million.

For the 2025 fiscal year, we have results for the first two quarters. An increase in average active rail cars and higher rates pushed revenue up for the Rail North America segment to $589 million compared to the $535.4 million the company reported the previous year. That was instrumental in pushing overall revenue for the company up from $766.6 million in the first half of 2024 to $852.1 million at the same time this year.

In its international business, GATX reported a rise in revenue from $168.9 million to $184.3 million. This came as the average active rail car has grown slightly from 28,198 to 28,572. But again, higher lease rates accounted for the lion’s share of the growth. There was also some growth when it came to the Engine Leasing segment, with sales climbing from $43.1 million to $58.2 million. All of this seems to have stemmed from aircraft spare engines acquired last year that were utilized in the engine capacity agreement that the company has with Rolls-Royce.

Net income for the company also jumped, rising from $118.7 million to $154.1 million. We saw operating cash flow expand from $235.9 million to $285.5 million. On an adjusted basis, the increase was from $251.7 million to $292 million. And finally, EBITDA for the business grew from $430.2 million to $494.1 million. For the current fiscal year, as a whole, management expects earnings per share of between $8.50 and $8.90. At the midpoint, we would be looking at net profits of $312.3 million compared to the $284.2 million reported for 2024. It's also looking like adjusted operating cash flow will be somewhere around $699.5 million, while EBITDA will be around $1.03 billion.

At the start of this article, I mentioned that management made a rather interesting maneuver earlier this year. Specifically, this was the firm's decision to partner up with institutional firms, namely Brookfield Infrastructure Partners L.P. (BIP), that involve a joint venture formed between the parties that will then subsequently acquire the operating lease portfolio of railcars currently owned by a subsidiary of Wells Fargo & Company (WFC). This is a massive transaction with a value of $4.4 billion.

Initially, ownership interest in this joint venture will be 30% for GATX and 70% for Brookfield. However, GATX will manage the railcars of the joint venture, as well as all railcars and locomotives wholly owned by Brookfield. The details are rather complex, but in short, over time, GATX has the opportunity to acquire up to 100% of the joint venture.

The structure of all of this is rather intriguing. For instance, 70% of the purchase price of the venture will come from committed debt financing of the joint venture which will unfortunately be guaranteed by GATX. The other 30% will be paid for through the two enterprises putting in $1.3 billion in exchange for equity in it.

This deal is not likely to close until early next year. Unfortunately, we don't really have much in the way of further information to gauge how profitable it might be. We do know that third-party estimates peg the growth in annual EBITDA associated with this maneuver as amounting to between 10% and 15% for the business. But because we don't have more concrete figures than this, I think it would be best to value the company excluding the prospect of growth here.

In the chart above, you can see how shares of the company are currently priced using estimates for 2025 and historical results for 2024. On an absolute basis, the stock looks decent but far from great. In the table below, I then compared it to four other similar enterprises. What I found was that, on a price-to-earnings basis, two of the four companies ended up being cheaper. This number increases to three of the four companies on a price-to-operating-cash-flow basis. But when it came to the EV-to-EBITDA approach, our candidate was the most expensive of the group.

Company Price/Earnings Price/Operating Cash Flow EV/EBITDA
GATX Corporation 18.3 8.1 13.4
Trinity Industries, Inc. (TRN) 24.5 5.6 11.7
The Greenbrier Companies, Inc. (GBX) 6.5 4.0 6.1
Wabash National Corporation (WNC) 5.9 4.1 4.4
FreightCar America, Inc. (RAIL) 978.0 8.3 6.3

Takeaway

Based on the data currently available, I believe that GATX is an interesting business. But it's not one that I am particularly enthusiastic about from an investment perspective. Shares are decently priced, but far from cheap. The stock actually looks a bit expensive compared to other similar firms. Yes, management is achieving nice growth, but it's not enough, in my opinion, to justify a bullish outlook at this time. Therefore, I have no problem keeping it rated a "Hold" for now.

data.title: Woori Financial: Stay The Course On Korea's Banking Laggard
data.description:
Woori Financial Group Inc. (NYSE:WF) is one of Korea’s ‘big four’ financial holding companies, with subsidiaries spanning Woori Card, Woori Bank, Woori Asset Management, Woori Investment Securities, and, most recently, life insurance (via the Tong Yang and ABL acquisitions). Since I last covered the name, Woori has continued its scorching year-to-date rally, along with the rest of Korea’s outperforming banking sector.

That said, the sector-wide rally has been somewhat interrupted in recent weeks by factors beyond Woori’s control. Namely, slightly disappointing (but still much improved) tax reforms by the new government and potential new regulatory fines. Plus, a key tenet of the Woori thesis that I have written about previously, the stock’s ‘deep value’, isn’t necessarily as compelling now following this year’s massive re-rate.

The key question then, now that Q2 earnings are done and two non-bank subsidiaries have been bolted on, is how much upside (if any) remains for Woori at these levels.

Defending Margins in the Face of Interest Rate Cuts

Korean interest rates are on the decline, so Woori’s resilient Q2 2025 numbers were a very positive outcome. In fact, the core banking business even saw some net interest margin expansion (+1bp for bank and card). On the revenue side, loan growth picked up thanks to an accelerating retail segment, which more than offset weakness in corporates.

Woori also tapped into cost levers to defend its margin – excluding one-offs, operating expenses were down 10.2% for the quarter. The only blemish for Woori Bank was credit costs, which rose to 49 bps on “preemptive provisioning for completion guaranteed trust projects”. Excluding this one-off, credit cost would have held firm. With management also guiding toward full-year credit costs staying within the low-to-mid 40 bps range, things look manageable enough.

The big headline positive for earnings, though, was non-interest income. Thanks to some very strong market conditions in Korea, as well as FX translation gains during the quarter, Woori saw a +47% quarter-on-quarter gain. Still, it’s important to keep in mind that most of this is non-recurring; instead, I’d focus on the resilience of Woori's core bank margins as the key positive takeaway.

Better Capitalized for Shareholder Returns

Perhaps the bigger positive, though, was that Woori is making good on its promise for 2025 to be “the year of capital ratio improvement”. Helped by a resilient P&L (+40 bps) and, to a lesser extent, FX (+15 bps), the group’s CET1 ratio improved to 12.76%. For context, this is above management’s 12.5% full-year target (post dividend) and puts Woori well on the path to reaching 13.0% well ahead of its end-2027 target.

A better capitalized balance sheet is important because it adds room for a big step up in capital returns (dividend + buybacks). The intent from management has also been positive, with both Woori’s dividend (+11%) and buybacks (+10%) already tracking well above last year’s “record high level”.

As for the longer term, management has already targeted a higher dividend payout ratio of 30%, as well as increased share buybacks/cancellations as part of its 50% “mid to long-term total shareholder return target”. All in all, a good setup for Woori’s next “corporate value enhancement” update.

Tax Reforms Boost Dividend Appeal

Externally, the new Korean government’s effort to reform the tax code also represents a tailwind for high-yielding Woori (~5% trailing dividend + high-single digits including buybacks).

While not quite on par with the market’s optimism (hence, Woori’s consolidation in recent weeks), the proposed revision still brings the top-end dividend income tax rate down to 35% for incomes above KRW300m - ten points off the current tax levy of 15-45%.

In addition to a lower tax burden, this amendment also breaks out dividend income from a catch-all “comprehensive income tax”. The caveat is that companies also need to meet a “high-dividend” classification, which entails having a payout ratio of 25% and a +5% rise in the cash dividend vs the trailing three-year average, to qualify. Not too many Korean corporates meet this threshold, so those that do, like Woori (~29% payout in FY24), stand to benefit disproportionately.

Non-Bank Catalyst in the Pipeline

Woori’s earnings have historically been driven by its core banking business, but management is actively diversifying into non-bank income streams. Firstly, from the brokerage business, which got up and running in H1; secondly, from two life insurance acquisitions, Tongyang and ABL Life, both of which are now incorporated as subsidiaries (as of July).

Financially, management is still “reevaluating all assets and liabilities held by the two companies at fair value”, so both insurers’ earnings have yet to kick in. The strategic direction of both businesses (“whether it will be merged or whether we will have 100%-owned subsidiaries”) hasn’t yet been decided either.

We are due for some kind of guidance, though, in terms of both the financial impact and business strategy, with earnings set to kick in from H2 2025. Expect this to catalyze upward revisions to Street estimates in the coming months.

As for the longer term, non-bank accretion really depends on the extent to which these businesses (brokerage + life insurance) lift Woori’s return on equity (or ROE) profile. Insurance, for instance, looks set to boost earnings but will also come with integration costs and “some impact” on capitalization levels.

The good news for investors is that the bar is very low. At ~0.5x forward book (~0.6x trailing) against a ~9% ‘steady state’ ROE for legacy Woori and a 10% “mid to long-term” target, the market isn’t pricing in very much non-bank success at all. Thus, any evidence of an ROE uplift could re-rate the stock quite significantly.

Summing Up

The easiest money might have already been made now that Woori has rallied over 60% this year, but a review of its fundamentals suggests there’s still upside potential – especially with the stock priced at only half of its forward book. In the meantime, keep an eye out for catalysts on the non-bank side, regulatory developments, and shareholder returns. All of these could catalyze a further narrowing of Woori’s deep valuation discount.

data.title: Genesco Cannot Grow Profitably, And Yet Trades At 20x Earnings
data.description:
Genesco Inc. (NYSE:GCO) recently reported Q2 '26 (calendar 2Q25) results. The results were not good, with revenue comps up MSD, but gross margins deteriorating and larger operating losses than a year ago.

I continue to believe the name is overvalued, trading at almost $30, versus an earnings guidance for the year of $1.50. 20x for a retailer in this context is simply too expensive. I maintain a Hold.

Q2 '26 results

Top line still positive: Genesco posted its 4th consecutive quarter of top-line positive comps (at 4%).

In particular, Journeys grew 9% comps, with the company commenting on the call that it is growing double digits in early Q3 '26 (Q3 '25 calendar). The brand is growing on top of growth, because Journeys also grew DD in Q3 '25 (Q3 '24 calendar).

Schuh, on the other hand, remains very challenged, with comps down 4%, despite higher markdowns and promotions.

The cost of growth: The problem with the above growth is that gross margins are down 100bps, because of markdowns and more promotionality.

The company's sales increased $20 million, but gross profits increased only $5 million, or 25% on the margin, versus gross margins of 46% for the quarter. That means the company's new sales (or the aggregate of sales) are much less profitable than the company's regular sales.

SG&A delever on a gross basis: Gross profits increased by $5 million, but SG&A increased by $10 million. The figure levered against sales by barely 20 bps, but in reality, it delevered against business profitability.

SG&A should have leveraged more because it holds the occupancy expenses, potentially the most leverageable part of a retailer's cost structure. However, the company spent more on marketing.

Operating income is worse than last year: What is surprising about the results is that despite the revenue growth, the company lost more money operationally than last year, both on a Q2 '26 or H1 '26 basis.

In fact, for Q2 '26, operating losses widened by 50%, from $10 million to almost $15 million.

The idea I get from all of this is that the company is buying sales with more promotions and higher advertising expenses. This is good for results releases showing growth, but it doesn't bring home more profitability.

Hopes for H2 '26

The company maintained guidance for EPS at $1.3/1.7 for the year. Given it has generated losses of more than $40 million in H1 '26, it means it expects H2 '26, in particular the key Back To School and Holidays seasons, to deliver the profitability for the year. $1.7 in EPS implies a net profit of $18 million, or basically more than $60 million in operating income in H2 '26.

Valuation is too high

Genesco is growing MSD, which is growth but not impressive, and shows signs that such growth cannot be done profitably, as promotions and advertising eat those profits. Indeed, growth leads to lower (negative) operating margins.

A company that cannot grow profits should not command a high earnings multiple. However, Genesco currently trades at almost $30 but is guiding for EPS of $1.5 for the year, which implies a 20x earnings multiple.

Not only this, there are so far no signs that the guidance is achievable as the company is not leveraging, and may need to over-discount in the key holiday sales seasons.

I believe the name is clearly not an opportunity and can even be considered overvalued. For this reason, I maintain my Hold rating.

data.title: SPS Commerce: Victim Of Trade Uncertainty
data.description:
In June, I believed that SPS Commerce (NASDAQ:SPSC) suffered from a slower pace of growth, creating a more compelling situation. Following a 25% pullback observed at the time, I was growing more compelled to the shares, but did not see appeal.

This came as much of the growth was driven by M&A efforts and adjusted for stock-based compensation expenses, earnings were not that impressive, as I concluded to revisit this stance if shares would move closer to the $100 mark.

A $140 stock at the time has fallen another 20% over the summer, moving down on the back of the announcement of the second quarter earnings report and comments about its outlook, making it opportune to revisit the investment case here.

Electronic Data Integration

SPS Commerce is a provider of EDI solutions, with EDI standing for Electronic Data Integration, used for electronic data integration between trading partners and stakeholders across supply chains operated by retailers and other (retail) groups.

The idea is to share information to facilitate quick delivery, optimal planning, logistics, and inventory management, all aimed to minimize the time and maximize the efficiency of the route traveled from producers to shelf.

The company provides such services to more than 100,000 companies, offering various functions related to fulfillment, compliance and others. A $150 million business in the mid 2010s has increased sales by a factor of 5 times to about three quarters of a billion, while the organic and inorganic growth story came at the expense of "only" 15% dilution, while real margin gains were delivered upon.

Boosting M&A

SPS Commerce has been quite acquisitive over the past year, including a $206 million purchase of SupplyPike in the summer of last year, an expensive deal with the automated invoice deduction management software business generating just $25 million in annual sales. The company furthermore announced a $210 million deal to acquire Carbon6 Technologies early in 2024, with the company providing software solutions to Amazon sellers, which came at a lower sales multiple, with the revenue contribution pegged at $40 million, for a combined $65 million revenue contribution supporting current sales growth.

In February of this year, the company reported a 19% increase in 2024 sales to $638 million, with GAAP operating profits reported at $89 million, this number was impacted by a $23 million amortization charge. For the year, GAAP earnings were posted at $2.04 per share, with adjusted earnings reported at $3.48 per share, yet as much of the discrepancy stemmed from a $54 million stock-based compensation expense, realistic earnings only trending around $2.50 per share.

For the year 2025, the company guided for sales to increase between 19-20% to $758-$763 million, with adjusted earnings seen between $3.78-$3.84 per share. This $0.33 per share improvement in adjusted earnings looks promising, yet stock-based compensation were seen increasing to $63 million as well, which really suggests that if we take this expense out, earnings would really be stagnant.

In April, the company reported a solid 21% increase in first quarter sales to $181 million, with adjusted earnings reported at $1.00 per share, with full-year earnings now seen between $3.86 and $3.93 per share, with realistic earnings seen around $3 per share if we back out stock-based compensation expenses. Trading at $140, the company was valued at $5.3 billion, a valuation including a $95 million net cash position.

At such levels, multiples were still demanding at $140, for an earnings multiple in the mid-forties. This was a too demanding valuation multiple in my view given that half of the 20% growth as the result of M&A efforts, as growth was slowing down, also due to trade complications induced by tariffs. With valuations being too demanding, I was hoping for a continuation of growth and/or pullback to reconsider this stance.

Coming Down

After trading around the $140 mark in June, shares plunged to levels seen near the $100 mark by the end of July, upon the release of the second quarter earnings report. Shares hit a low of $102 per share and ever since have recovered to $111 per share at this moment of writing.

Second quarter sales grew as much as 22% to $187 million, a slight acceleration in the pace of growth, aided by the purchase of Carbon6 which closed in February, with adjusted earnings reported at $1.00 per share, in line with the first quarter. GAAP earnings only came in at $0.52 per share. Stock-based compensation expense this quarter totaled $15 million on a pre-tax basis, or about $0.30 per share if we assume statutory tax rates, for realistic earnings around $0.70 per quarter, or $2.80 per share for the year.

In this light, the third quarter guidance felt a bit soft, with sales seen up 17-18% to $192-$193 million, with adjusted earnings seen between $0.96-$1.00 per share, with stock-based compensation expenses furthermore seen up to $16 million for the quarter. For the year, sales are seen around $761 million, with adjusted earnings at the midpoint seen topping $4 per share.

Trading down to $111, the market value of the firm has fallen to $4.27 billion, including a $107 million net cash position, for a $4.16 billion operating asset valuation. Based on the full-year outlook, this valued the business just over 5 times sales here, and with cash holdings equal to about $3 per share, unleveraged assets trade at a realistic 38 times earnings multiple based on realistic earnings power around $2.80 per share.

What Now?

Frankly, the lower pace of growth does not surprise me given the macroeconomic economic conditions, as the high multiple left shares vulnerable, as all that exactly happened over the summer. Perhaps some investors were betting on a more muted impact amidst macro uncertainty, as the solutions of SPS Commerce might be even more valuable in choppy trading times, yet its customers are clearly not in spending/investment mode right now.

Right now, earnings multiples have come in from about 45 times to 38 times, largely driven by the multiple coming in, which is still a high multiple, all while realistic earnings are a bit lower than I anticipated as well. Amidst all this, investors are fearful, amidst an uncertain trading environment, slower growth, and higher stock-based compensation.

Another concern is that of comments from management on the conference call that revenue growth is seen coming down to high single digits beyond 2025, being somewhat of a warning into 2026. This might have overshadowed comments that the company sees room for EBITDA margins to increase by another 2 points per annum, which frankly looks quite ambitious and encouraging.

In between all this, multiples have come in and appeal has improved, yet that's still not the same as appeal is to be seen, as I'm lowering my desired entry target to levels sub-$100, as I look forward with greater interest to keep an eye on the shares here.

data.title: Boeing 737 MAX Nears Key Milestone
data.description:
The Boeing Company (NYSE:BA) has strong momentum this year with a revival of order inflow and production, as I discussed in a prior report. According to a news item by Seeking Alpha, the company indicated that it will raise the production of the best-selling Boeing 737 to 42 airplanes per month, effectively lifting the FAA cap that did not allow production to increase above 38 airplanes per month. In this report, I will discuss what a production rate increase to 42 airplanes per month means for Boeing in terms of ramping up production and revenues.

Before I run my analysis, I want to summarize several items from a prior report on the Boeing 737 MAX topic:

  • Boeing’s backlog comfortably supports production rates of 63 airplanes per month without any order inflow.
  • With a fourth line to be installed in Everett, the company will have an annual production capacity for the Boeing 737 MAX of 84 airplanes per month.
  • In the absence of a rate hike this year, realizing year-on-year growth on program revenues would be challenging.
  • A rate hike by mid-year would have resulted in a 9% growth in revenues in 2026.

These assumptions will be revised as needed. I also adjusted the rate ramp-up for the baseline assumption, eliminating a possible gap between increases and production that was too harsh.

For the Boeing 737 MAX models, the backlog has climbed from more than 4,700 airplanes to more than 4,850 airplanes. In my calculations, I use a backlog equivalent of 5 to 7 years. This currently supports a monthly production rate of 58 to 81 airplanes. Implementing the ASC606 adjustments for 470 jets would reduce it to 52 to 72 airplanes per month. If we use the reported backlog and reduce the inventory, we get to a supported rate of 57 to 80 airplanes, averaging 68.5 airplanes per month compared to an average of 66.5 airplanes per month in my prior assessment. This is on the assumption that the additional criteria Boeing has to count airplanes to the backlog are met and subsequently result in the ASC606 orders being added back to the inventory.

Boeing plans on opening a new line in Everett dedicated to the Boeing 737 MAX 10. The second analysis I ran kept in mind this additional line and respective backlogs.

This would result in support for 15-18 MAX 10 deliveries per month and 42-59 deliveries for the other MAX models. Essentially, this provides support for a production rate of around 68.5 airplanes per month, with safe support of around 65 airplanes per month. This is an increase of two airplanes per month, indicating that support from the backlog for higher production rates has increased.

Boeing Takes The Road In Between On Production Hikes

I previously modeled the accelerated production rate increase scenario as an increase in production in June this year. However, for future years, it contained a modeling deficiency that would result in full-line utilization being reached in 2033. With the renewed modeling applied, this shifts to 2030. Similarly, the baseline has been adjusted in the same way.

If we then look at what the current increase plan entails, namely a rate hike in October, we note that the next rate hike is set to occur six months later in April 2026, a six-month cadence. So, Boeing will be producing at three different rates each year until the full rate is reached in 2030-2031. We note that the three-month shift has no major implications for the full rate being reached, but in the near term, it substantially affects the company’s ability to ramp production. A relaxed schedule would see Boeing reaching full production capacity by 2031 (instead of the previously modeled full rate in 2034). In terms of the timeline to reach full rate on all four lines, there is no substantial change as all scenarios have Boeing reaching full rate around 2030-2031.

Boeing 737 MAX Revenues Can Grow To $50 Billion

Depending on how many airplanes Boeing can deliver, we note that the increase in October will add 2% to Boeing 737 revenues this year compared to the baseline and 6% next year. In terms of year-on-year revenue generation, the increase in October will drive an additional 5 percentage points to year-on-year growth in 2025 to 102%, but it will be flattish in the year after. It will give Boeing a more appreciable 6% growth on the program in 2026.

In terms of ramping up production, the three-month acceleration to the schedule won't do much in terms of driving additional growth relative to the prior year. In fact, from 2027 onwards, the baseline would see some higher relative growth compared to the accelerated production plan. This is driven by the acceleration in deliveries having more of an impact on 2025 and 2026 than in the years beyond, giving the base case scenario a favorable comparable base.

The renewed modeling approach on the baseline revenue generation also shows a significantly smaller differential in delivery value through 2031, when full-rate production will be achieved. This differential is around 1.7%. It simply means that from a financial perspective, in the long term, Boeing does not need to push production higher. It also does not have to do it for a substantial improvement in the years in which the earlier ramp-up drives growth most. The acceleration is more of a sign of Boeing being ready for it than that it has to drive up production now rather than later for the financial prospects. The difference in the baseline scenario and the rate hike in October drives around $3.6 billion in additional sales through 2027, and while that may seem huge, it actually is not regarding the total revenues and the need for Boeing to get the program rate increase right.

Conclusion: Boeing Is On The Right Track

Boeing initially targeted to demonstrate stability in rates for six months before going up in production. With the rate increase in October, they are deviating from that. That is something that not all shareholders may like if this decision is being made driven by financials.

At the same time, it should be noted that a rate increase also signals confidence by management, and if the FAA also approves the rate increase, it is a sign of confidence in Boeing’s KPI framework to keep a healthy production standard with quality, supply chain, and safety in mind. The production level of 42 airplanes per month is also a somewhat easier step for Boeing to make, as it is equivalent to the production of two lines, and Boeing did not open the third line in Renton until it started working on the Boeing 737 MAX. So, Boeing has a lot of experience with the Boeing 737 Next Generation for production of 42 airplanes per month, and that means that stepping to 42 airplanes per month in some sense is a lower-risk step that can be made earlier than the six-month cadence. I now also see that between the baseline scenario and the scenario of a rate increase in October, there is no major long-term positive impact for revenue generation. We are talking about less than a 2% acceleration in revenues.

I believe that the rate hike comes at a moment when Boeing is ready for it, not because it desperately needs to show growth to Wall Street.

data.title: Nebius Group: My Update On Avride's Fair Value
data.description:
Background

Since my previous article on the valuation of Avride (a subsidiary of Nebius Group N.V. (NASDAQ:NBIS)), more than half a year has passed. During this time, the robotaxi market has seen significant developments — both in terms of business progress by the leading players and in their market valuations. Given these changes, I decided to update my valuation of Avride. To do so, I will first walk through the key news flow over the period, review the current competitive landscape in the U.S., and then update my valuation of Avride and, consequently, of Nebius Group as a whole.

Let’s start with Waymo.

Waymo

Key Metrics

Waymo remains the clear leader in the U.S. robotaxi market. The majority stake in Waymo is held by Alphabet (GOOGL).

In July, the company surpassed 10 million fully autonomous rides, compared to just 5 million at the beginning of the year. Notably, 4 million of those rides occurred in 2024 alone, while only 1 million were completed before 2024. At the current pace, Waymo is on track to reach at least 15 million rides by year-end.

Total autonomous miles driven recently passed the 100 million mark (versus 64 million at the beginning of the year). In addition, Waymo crossed the 200,000 weekly paid rides threshold in February and reached 250,000 per week by April — up from just 100,000 in August 2024. With its ongoing geographic expansion, a run rate of 300,000 weekly paid rides appears imminent.

Geographic Footprint

  • Los Angeles: Available through the Waymo One app. In the past six months, the service area expanded to 120 square miles, with initial freeway testing underway. Fleet: 400+ vehicles.

  • Phoenix: Accessible via Uber and Waymo One. The city has Waymo’s largest coverage area: 315 square miles, with a fleet of 300+ vehicles.

  • San Francisco: Available through Waymo One. Coverage has grown from 55 sq. miles (earlier this year) to 130 sq. miles. Waymo also obtained a permit to map routes to San Francisco International Airport — one of the most commercially attractive corridors. Fleet: 600+ vehicles.

New Cities (Austin & Atlanta)

Last year, Waymo announced plans to launch commercial operations in Austin and Atlanta in partnership with Uber in 2025. Austin went live in March 2025 with a 37 sq. mile coverage area and ~100 vehicles, while Atlanta followed in June with 65 sq. miles and a fleet currently only in the dozens but expected to scale into the hundreds.

Overall, Waymo now operates in five U.S. metro areas with a combined population of 7.7 million.

2026 Plans

Waymo plans to launch commercial service in Miami, Dallas, and Washington, D.C. next year, while also adding 10 new test locations in 2025. Of note: testing is expected to start in Philadelphia, and the company has received a permit for trials in New York City.

Internationally, Waymo began pilot operations in Tokyo in partnership with Nihon Kotsu and Go. Commercial launch in Tokyo is expected next year.

Fleet & Manufacturing

Waymo’s commercial fleet now exceeds 1,500 vehicles. The company is also expanding production capacity:

  • In Phoenix, Arizona, Waymo and Magna are ramping up a new facility that will build up to 2,000 Jaguar I-Pace–based robotaxis in 2026. The plant is designed to support Zeekr RT–based robotaxis as well. At full capacity, it could produce tens of thousands of vehicles annually.

  • Separately, a Detroit facility is expected to begin assembling Hyundai IONIQ 5–based robotaxis next year.

These initiatives suggest Waymo’s fleet will scale significantly in the coming years.

Revenue Prospects and Valuation

At the current run rate of 250,000 weekly paid rides, and assuming an average fare of $10 per ride, Waymo’s revenue ARR (weekly revenue multiple by 52) already exceeds $130 million. By year-end, ARR could surpass $200 million.

Waymo’s last funding round closed in October 2024, raising $5.6 billion at a $45 billion valuation. Since then, no new financing rounds have been disclosed.

Challengers for the #2 Spot

After Cruise exited the market, there is still no clear second player in U.S. robotaxis, though several contenders are vying for the position. As a remainder, Cruise was previously the #2 player behind Waymo. General Motors (GM) acquired the company in 2016 for $581 million, and Cruise had been rapidly narrowing the gap with Waymo. However, after an accident in October 2023, Cruise suspended all road testing and recalled its vehicles. In December 2024, GM unexpectedly announced that it would shut down Cruise’s robotaxi operations entirely and reallocate its technology toward GM's ADAS improvement. The only notable update since my last coverage was GM’s move to cut 50% of Cruise’s staff — a predictable outcome following the wind-down.

Tesla

In June, Tesla (TSLA) finally launched its robotaxi service in Austin. The company plans to introduce a dedicated robotaxi model (unveiled at the “We, Robot” event in 2024), though production won’t start until 2026. Until then, operations are running on the Model Y.

Tesla’s key advantage lies in scale: unlike other AV players, Tesla does not need to retrofit vehicles with LIDARs and multiple sensors. Its autonomous stack relies solely on the company’s camera-based vision system.

However, Tesla’s service is far from Waymo’s in maturity:

  • All rides in Austin currently require a safety driver.

  • Access is by invitation only.

The company has applied for permits to launch in Phoenix and is exploring a limited rollout in San Francisco, but these efforts remain early stage.

Zoox (Amazon)

Founded in 2014 and acquired by Amazon (AMZN) in 2020, Zoox has accelerated development since the acquisition. While it tests its AV stack on Toyota Highlander platforms, the company is betting on its purpose-built robotaxi, showcased at CES 2025. Zoox is currently testing in eight cities:

  • Autonomous, non-commercial pilots: San Francisco, Las Vegas, Foster City.

  • Test with safety driver behind the wheel: Atlanta, Los Angeles, Austin, Miami, Seattle.

In Las Vegas, the service is already available to early access users, with plans to go fully commercial by year-end.

On the manufacturing side, Zoox opened a new facility in Hayward, California in June with a peak capacity of 10,000 AVs per year. This complements its earlier plant near Fremont.

Challenges remain: Zoox recalled vehicles several times in 2025 due to safety incidents, including hard-braking issues (258 vehicles recalled in March), a collision with a passenger car in Las Vegas (270 vehicles recalled in April), and a May accident involving an e-scooter. These issues underscore that Zoox’s technology still needs refinement to avoid a fate similar to Cruise.

Uber + Lucid + Nuro

Uber’s (UBER) interest in robotaxis is well known. After selling its AV unit (Uber ATG) to Aurora (AUR), Uber shifted toward a partnership-based model, currently boasting around 20 AV-related collaborations.

In July, Uber surprised the market by announcing a partial return to AV development via partnership with Lucid Motors and Nuro:

  • Lucid Motors (LCID): Uber will invest $300 million and commit to purchasing 20,000 Lucid Gravity EVs over six years.

  • Nuro: Uber will invest several hundred million dollars. Nuro, previously focused on delivery robots, pivoted in April to licensing its AV technology to automakers. The post-money valuation after the July financing round was $6 billion, down ~30% from its $8.6 billion peak valuation in 2021.

Prototype AVs from these partnerships are already being tested at a proving ground in Las Vegas.

Lyft

Like Uber, Lyft (LYFT) once had its own AV division but sold it to Woven Planet (Toyota) in 2021. Since then, it has pursued partnerships — with limited success:

  • Argo AI (Ford + Volkswagen JV) collapsed in 2022.

  • Motional (Hyundai + Aptiv JV) abandoned commercial robotaxi development in 2024, cut 40% of staff, and was consolidated by Hyundai.

Lyft has since pivoted back toward its robotaxi strategy:

  • Plans to introduce autonomous shuttles from Austria’s Benteler in 2026.

  • New partnerships with May Mobility and Mobileye:

    • Launch in Dallas with Mobileye expected in 2026.

    • Commercial robotaxi service in Peachtree Corners, Georgia with May Mobility expected before year-end.

May Mobility

In addition to its Lyft partnership, May Mobility announced a collaboration with Uber to launch a service in Arlington, Texas by late 2025. The fleet will use Toyota Sienna Vehicles, initially with safety drivers.

Notable milestones:

  • December 2023: launched service in Sun City, Arizona (later discontinued).

  • November 2024: launched a small fleet in Ann Arbor, Michigan.

Mobileye

Mobileye (MBLY) is testing its Drive technology in Austin, Detroit, and Orlando. It is also running pilots in Norway, Germany, and Israel to assess system performance in extreme weather conditions. However, the company’s most advanced commercial launch plan is in Dallas, where it intends to go live with Lyft in 2026.

Wayve

Wayve is primarily a European AV developer, with most of its testing in the UK. In October 2024, however, the company began pilots in San Francisco using Ford’s Mustang Mach-E platform.

Founded in 2017, Wayve — like Tesla — pursues an AI- and vision-only approach to autonomy without LIDAR. While behind U.S. peers in commercialization, Wayve has raised $1.05 billion in May 2024 at a $2.78 billion post-money valuation.

Recent news: Wayve signed a partnership with Uber to launch a London robotaxi service in the coming years. The regulatory framework should allow tests by spring 2026, though the underlying vehicle platform has not been disclosed. One possibility is Nissan (OTCPK:NSANY), which announced in April 2025 that it would integrate Wayve’s software into its ADAS stack starting in 2027.

Volkswagen

Volkswagen (OTCPK:VWAGY) ADMT, a subsidiary launched in July 2023 (nine months after the Argo AI shutdown), is also building out robotaxi operations. Testing began in Austin with 10 ID. Buzz EVs equipped with Mobileye hardware.

Commercial rollout is planned for Los Angeles in 2026, in partnership with Uber. Initially, operations will require a safety driver, with fully driverless service expected by 2027.

Chinese Robotaxi Developers

Chinese AV players remain unlikely to enter the U.S. market in the near future. For Avride, this means limited competitive pressure outside the U.S. — for now, its only meaningful international testing is in South Korea, where it has a nationwide permit for AV pilots (with safety drivers required).

One potential wildcard: Travis Kalanick (Uber’s founder) is reportedly exploring the acquisition of Pony AIi’s U.S. business, which could add another competitor stateside.

Avride

Avride is not new to the AV industry. Before the Yandex N.V. restructuring, it actively developed robotaxis and delivery robots in Russia, Israel, and the U.S. After the split, much of the Yandex Self-Driving Group team joined Avride. Development slowed during the sale process of the Russian business but has since reaccelerated.

Since then, Avride has launched several delivery partnerships:

  • Uber Eats – courier robots in Austin, Dallas, and Jersey City.

  • Grubhub – over 100 courier robots at Ohio State University.

  • Rakuten – delivery pilots in Tokyo.

  • Mitsui Fudosan – robot delivery service at Mitsui Outlet Park Kisarazu (Japan’s largest outlet mall).

As in my previous article, I will not dwell on the delivery business, as the commercial opportunity is far smaller than robotaxis.

Avride signed agreements with Uber and Hyundai to launch robotaxis based on the Hyundai IONIQ 5 in Dallas by year-end 2025. If successful, Avride would become the first major player in Dallas (pop. 1.3M), ahead of Waymo and Lyft-Mobileye, both scheduled to enter in 2026.

Avride’s safety track record is also noteworthy: since its launch in 2017, it has reported zero serious incidents (injuries or major vehicle damage) over 22 million kilometers driven. Its fleet stood at 10–20 vehicles in 2024 but is expected to scale to 100+ vehicles in 2025.

From a technology perspective, Avride, according to my etimates, ranks second only to Waymo in the U.S. market. Commercially, however, its fleet size and geographic footprint lag far behind Waymo. Still, Avride has a realistic path to becoming the #2 or #3 U.S. robotaxi player (depending on Tesla’s execution).

The Uber deal is clearly a positive, but it is non-exclusive. Uber already partners with 20 AV companies. I think Lyft could also emerge as a future partner, depending on the limitstions of Avride’s current Uber agreement.

Avride's Financials and Capital Requirements

Avride’s financials disclosed in Nebius Group’s 2024 Form 20-F show:

  • Revenue: $0.3 million.

  • Adjusted EBITDA loss: $67 million

Such losses are typical for a growth-stage AV developer. According to Nebius's presentation, Avride requires $150 million of funding through late 2025 to scale operations. These figures align with fleet expansion plans for both robotaxis and delivery robots.

According to Nebius CEO Arkady Volozh, the parent company will provide this funding. However, larger-scale commercialization would require additional investment. Nebius does not rule out selling a minority or even majority stake in Avride, similar to prior carve-outs of ClickHouse and Toloka.

As I argued previously, the most likely candidates are Uber and Hyundai, with possible participation from Lyft, European OEMs, or venture firms. A new possibility is Nvidia, which could invest directly in Avride in addition to its stake in Nebius Group.

Avride's Valuation

Company Last valuation Date of the last valuation

Waymo

 $45 000 million October 2024
Cruise $30 000 million January 2021
Motional $4 070 million May 2024
Zoox $1 300 million June 2020
Wayve $2 780 million May 2024
Pony.ai $4 690 million August 2025
WeRide $1 990 million August 2025
Nuro $6 000 million July 2025

Source: created by the author using companies' press releases and seeking alpha data.

For comparables, I exclude Waymo (too far ahead), Cruise and Zoox (outdated valuations), and WeRide/Pony.ai (different markets). The most relevant benchmarks are Motional, Wayve, and Nuro.

Previously, I averaged Motional and Wayve valuation, but I now view Nuro’s latest $6 billion valuation as the best reference point:

  • Avride’s technology is at least on par with Nuro.

  • Nuro investors could invest in Avride as well.

Thus, I estimate Avride’s fair value at no less than $6 billion.

Final Thoughts

One nuance I missed in my prior valuation: 17% of Avride’s equity (on a diluted basis) belongs to employees via derivative instruments. This leaves Nebius Group with an 83% stake.

At a $6 billion Avride valuation, Nebius’s stake is worth $4.98 billion — 46.5% higher than my prior estimate of $3.4 billion. While valuation is inherently uncertain, this represents the most reasonable current benchmark.

Avride 3.4 5

Net cash position 1.69 1.69
